summary: Jeff Atwood praises Paint.NET as a rare example of polished open source software and uses it to advocate for financially supporting software developers. He references his personal commitment to spend $20 monthly supporting independent developers and argues that open source projects should make donations easy. The post highlights how Paint.NET exemplifies best practices by including donation links in the installer and providing one-click payment options. Atwood concludes that every non-commercial project should ask for donations, as not everyone can contribute time but many want to vote with their wallets.
key_points: Paint.NET stands out as an exceptionally polished open source project with professional UI, installer, website, and documentation
Software users should financially support independent developers and small vendors, even when software is free
Open source projects often make the mistake of not providing easy ways to donate
Paint.NET demonstrates best practices by including donation prompts in the installer and offering one-click payment options
Supporting developers financially is a way of 'voting with your wallet' for the software ecosystem you want
money_quotes: Stop reading this post right now and buy that software.

If I can't come up with the scratch to spend a measly $20 a month supporting the very best work of my fellow independent software developers, can I really call myself a professional software developer?
I believe in the little guy writing cool Windows software. And by 'believe in,' I mean 'pay.'
If I want to vote with my wallet, make it easy for me to give you my money.
It's hugely important to ask for donations on any non-commercial project.
Not everyone can contribute time and effort. Help us help your project. Let us vote with our wallets.
I work extra hard to avoid reinforcing all these negative stereotypes.
key_insight: Open source projects should make it effortless for users to donate, because financial support is how many people can best contribute to software they value.
